---
title: "Chapter 40 — 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 39 · 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning](ch39.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [41 · 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics →](ch41.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Once an agent can *represent* the world, the next question is what to *do* in it. This chapter is about deliberate, search-based decision making: laying out a sequence of actions to reach a goal (planning), pinning down values that satisfy a web of restrictions (constraint satisfaction), and choosing moves against an opponent (game playing). These are the classical, symbolic heart of AI — methods that reason explicitly over states and rules rather than learning a policy from data, and they still power schedulers, solvers, verification tools, and game engines today.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Classical & Symbolic AI · used to decide sequences of actions, assign values under constraints, and play adversarial games · the one key idea: search a structured space of possibilities, using structure to prune the parts you never need to explore.
:::

## 40.1 — Automated planning: STRIPS, states, actions, goals

Imagine telling a robot "make me a cup of coffee" and having it figure out, on its own, that it must first find a mug, then fill it, then carry it to you. **Automated planning** is the problem of finding a sequence of actions that transforms an initial situation into one that satisfies a goal. Unlike a hand-coded script that lists the exact steps, a planner is given only a *model* of what actions are possible and reasons out the sequence itself — which means the same planner solves "make coffee" and "make tea" without reprogramming.

The classic formalism is **STRIPS** (Stanford Research Institute Problem Solver). A planning problem in STRIPS has four pieces. A **state** is a set of facts that are currently true, e.g. `{at(robot, kitchen), empty(mug)}` — and crucially, anything not listed is assumed false (the *closed-world assumption*). The **initial state** is where you start. The **goal** is a partial description of the states you want to reach, e.g. `{holds(mug), full(mug)}` — any state containing those facts counts, no matter what else is also true. An **action** (or operator) is described by three lists:

- **preconditions** — facts that must hold for the action to apply,
- **add list** — facts the action makes true,
- **delete list** — facts the action makes false.

This is the celebrated **STRIPS assumption**: an action changes only the facts in its add and delete lists; everything else carries over unchanged. That sidesteps the **frame problem** — the awkward need to state explicitly that picking up a mug does *not* change the color of the walls, the time of day, or a thousand other facts — by simply declaring that unmentioned facts persist.

Applying an action $a$ to a state $s$ is then pure set algebra:

$$ s' = (s \setminus \text{Del}(a)) \cup \text{Add}(a), \quad \text{defined only when } \text{Pre}(a) \subseteq s $$

Here is that transition encoded directly:

```python
def apply(state, action):
    # action = (name, pre, add, delete), each a set of facts
    _, pre, add, delete = action
    assert pre <= state, "preconditions not met"   # pre ⊆ state
    return (state - delete) | add                  # STRIPS update

start = {"at_kitchen", "mug_empty"}
fill  = ("fill", {"at_kitchen", "mug_empty"}, {"mug_full"}, {"mug_empty"})
assert apply(start, fill) == {"at_kitchen", "mug_full"}
```

In practice nobody writes raw STRIPS today; the lingua franca is **PDDL** (Planning Domain Definition Language), which separates a reusable *domain* file (the action schemas, written with variables like `?x`) from a *problem* file (the concrete objects, initial state, and goal). Solvers like Fast Downward read PDDL and return a plan. The payoff of this declarative split is huge: describe the physics of a domain once, then throw any number of problems at the same solver.

::: {.callout-tip}
The whole point of STRIPS is that planning becomes **search over states**, where actions are the edges between them. Everything in the search toolbox — breadth-first search, A\*, admissible heuristics — applies directly once you frame planning this way. A planner is mostly a graph search with an automatically generated heuristic.
:::

## 40.2 — Searching for plans: forward, backward, partial-order, hierarchical

Given the state-transition model above, how do you actually find a plan? There are several search *directions*, and the difference between them is genuinely strategic — it changes which parts of the space you ever look at.

**Forward (progression) search** starts at the initial state and applies actions to generate successors until it hits a state satisfying the goal. It is intuitive and every generated state is real and reachable, but the **branching factor** — the number of applicable actions per state — can be enormous, so blind forward search drowns in irrelevant options. Modern planners tame this with automatically derived heuristics. The most important is the **delete-relaxation** heuristic, which estimates remaining cost by pretending actions never delete anything: in the relaxed world facts only ever accumulate, which makes it easy to estimate how far the goal is, and that estimate guides A\* in the real world.

**Backward (regression) search** starts from the goal and works backwards: given a set of facts you want, find an action whose add list contributes one of them, and replace that fact with the action's preconditions (the new sub-goals you'd need first). It only ever considers actions *relevant* to the goal, pruning huge swaths of forward activity that could never matter — at the cost of reasoning over partial, sometimes inconsistent, sub-goal sets that may not correspond to any reachable state.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  I["Initial state"] -->|progression| F1[s1] --> F2[s2] --> G["Goal ✓"]
  G -.->|regression| R1["subgoal set"] -.-> R2["subgoal set"] -.-> I
```

Both of the above commit to a *total order* of actions — a single straight line of steps. But often the order between two actions genuinely does not matter: buttering the bread and boiling the kettle are independent, and forcing an arbitrary order just multiplies the states you search. **Partial-order planning (POP)** exploits this by building a plan as a graph of actions with ordering constraints added *only when necessary* — for example when one action's effect supplies another's precondition (a **causal link**), or when a third action would undo a fact some link depends on (a **threat**, resolved by ordering the troublemaker before or after the link, called *promotion* or *demotion*). This **least-commitment** strategy keeps options open as long as possible; the final plan is any topological sort of the resulting partial order.

**Hierarchical Task Network (HTN) planning** takes a different tack: instead of (or alongside) reasoning from first principles, it encodes human know-how as **methods** that decompose a high-level task into subtasks. "Make coffee" decomposes into "get mug", "brew", "pour"; "brew" decomposes further, recursively, until you reach primitive STRIPS actions that can be executed directly. HTN planners are extremely effective in practice — logistics, manufacturing, game AI — precisely because the decomposition library injects expert structure that a from-scratch planner would have to rediscover. The tradeoff is that the library has to be authored and maintained by a human.

| Approach | Searches from | Strength | Main cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward / progression | initial state | states are real; great heuristics exist | huge branching factor |
| Backward / regression | goal | only goal-relevant actions | partial states get messy |
| Partial-order | constraint set | parallelism, least commitment | bookkeeping of threats |
| Hierarchical (HTN) | top-level tasks | encodes expert know-how, scales | must author method library |

## 40.3 — Constraint Satisfaction Problems

Some problems aren't about sequences of actions at all — they're about finding one consistent *assignment*. Scheduling exams so no student sits two at once, coloring a map so neighbors differ, solving a Sudoku: in each, you must give every item a value such that a set of rules is respected, and the *path* you took to get there doesn't matter, only the final assignment. This is a **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)**, and framing a problem as a CSP unlocks a whole family of general-purpose solving techniques you'd otherwise have to reinvent.

A CSP has three parts:

- **Variables** $X_1, \dots, X_n$ — the things to assign (each region of the map, each empty Sudoku cell).
- **Domains** $D_1, \dots, D_n$ — the allowed values for each variable (the four colors; the digits 1–9).
- **Constraints** — rules restricting which *combinations* of values are allowed (adjacent regions differ; each row, column, and box has no repeats).

A **solution** is a complete assignment that violates no constraint. The classic illustration is **map coloring**: color the regions of a map so no two bordering regions share a color. With variables = regions, domains = available colors, and a constraint $X_i \neq X_j$ for every shared border, the famous result that any planar map needs at most four colors becomes a concrete CSP you can hand to a solver.

A useful mental model is the **constraint graph**: a node per variable, an edge between any two variables that share a constraint. Much of CSP solving is really reasoning over this graph — and its *shape* matters. A constraint graph that happens to be a tree, for instance, can be solved in time linear in the number of variables, no backtracking at all; it is the loops in the graph that make a CSP hard.

The key insight that makes CSPs powerful is that they carry **structure the solver can exploit**: constraints are explicit, so a solver can reason about which values are *impossible* before ever guessing them. That is the difference between blind trial-and-error and the techniques in the next two sections.

<svg viewBox="0 0 320 120" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <circle cx="60" cy="60" r="22" fill="#e07a5f"/><text x="60" y="65" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#fff">R1</text>
  <circle cx="160" cy="40" r="22" fill="#3d8a6b"/><text x="160" y="45" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#fff">R2</text>
  <circle cx="160" cy="100" r="22" fill="#3d6ea5"/><text x="160" y="105" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#fff">R3</text>
  <circle cx="260" cy="60" r="22" fill="#e07a5f"/><text x="260" y="65" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#fff">R4</text>
  <line x1="82" y1="55" x2="138" y2="44" stroke="#888" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="82" y1="66" x2="138" y2="96" stroke="#888" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="160" y1="62" x2="160" y2="78" stroke="#888" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="182" y1="44" x2="238" y2="55" stroke="#888" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="182" y1="96" x2="238" y2="66" stroke="#888" stroke-width="2"/>
</svg>

Adjacent regions must differ; R1 and R4 share no border, so they can safely reuse the same color.

## 40.4 — Solving CSPs: backtracking and constraint propagation

The brute-force way to solve a CSP is to try every combination of values — but with $n$ variables and $d$ values each, that's $d^n$ possibilities, hopeless for anything real. **Backtracking search** is the smarter baseline: assign one variable at a time, check consistency as you go, and the moment a partial assignment violates a constraint, undo the last choice (backtrack) and try the next value. It is depth-first search over partial assignments, and the win is that it abandons a doomed branch the instant it goes bad, instead of fleshing out a hopeless full assignment before noticing.

```python
def backtrack(assignment, csp):
    if len(assignment) == len(csp.vars):        # all assigned → done
        return assignment
    var = next(v for v in csp.vars if v not in assignment)   # pick unassigned
    for val in csp.domains[var]:
        if csp.consistent(var, val, assignment):             # no constraint broken
            assignment[var] = val
            result = backtrack(assignment, csp)
            if result: return result
            del assignment[var]                              # undo, try next
    return None                                              # dead end → backtrack
```

Plain backtracking still wastes effort rediscovering the same conflicts over and over. **Constraint propagation** fixes this by *inferring* the consequences of the current assignment and shrinking domains *before* guessing again. The lightest form is **forward checking**: the moment you assign a variable, delete now-inconsistent values from the domains of its unassigned neighbors; if any neighbor's domain empties, you know this branch is dead immediately, without descending into it.

A stronger and very famous form is **arc consistency**, computed by the **AC-3** algorithm. An "arc" $X \to Y$ is consistent when *every* value left in $X$'s domain has *at least one* compatible value in $Y$'s domain. AC-3 repeatedly removes values that have no such support, and — this is the clever part — whenever it prunes a domain it re-queues the arcs pointing *into* that variable, because removing a value might have destroyed support for a neighbor:

```python
def ac3(csp):
    queue = list(csp.arcs())                  # all directed arcs (Xi, Xj)
    while queue:
        xi, xj = queue.pop()
        if revise(csp, xi, xj):               # removed some value from Di?
            if not csp.domains[xi]:            # domain wiped out → no solution
                return False
            for xk in csp.neighbors[xi] - {xj}:
                queue.append((xk, xi))         # changes may cascade
    return True

def revise(csp, xi, xj):
    removed = False
    for x in set(csp.domains[xi]):
        # drop x if NO value in Dj is compatible with it
        if not any(csp.allowed(xi, x, xj, y) for y in csp.domains[xj]):
            csp.domains[xi].discard(x); removed = True
    return removed
```

In a Sudoku, propagation alone — "this cell can only be a 7, because every other digit already appears in its row, column, or box" — often solves easy boards outright, and dramatically thins the search tree on hard ones. The big practical levers layered on top are **ordering heuristics**: choose the next variable by **MRV** (minimum remaining values — attack the most constrained variable first, so you fail fast where failure is likely), and choose its value by **least-constraining-value** (pick the value that rules out the fewest options for its neighbors, leaving the most room to succeed). Together, propagation plus good ordering routinely turn an exponential nightmare into something solved in milliseconds.

::: {.callout-warning}
Arc consistency is a *pruning* step, not a solver. Making a CSP fully arc-consistent does **not** guarantee a solution exists, and a non-empty domain after AC-3 does **not** mean every remaining value participates in some solution. You still generally need backtracking search — propagation just makes each step of that search far cheaper.
:::

## 40.5 — SAT solving: the universal constraint engine

There is one CSP so fundamental it became the bedrock of the entire field: **Boolean satisfiability**, or **SAT**. The variables are simply true/false, and the question is whether some assignment of them makes a Boolean formula come out true. Formulas are written in **conjunctive normal form (CNF)** — an AND of **clauses**, where each clause is an OR of **literals**, and a literal is a variable or its negation, like $(x_1 \lor \lnot x_2) \land (\lnot x_1 \lor x_3)$. A clause is satisfied when at least one of its literals is true; the whole formula is satisfied when *every* clause is. So solving SAT means finding one true/false setting that makes every clause "light up".

SAT matters out of all proportion to its simplicity, for two reasons. First, it was the original **NP-complete** problem (the Cook–Levin theorem): an astonishing range of problems — scheduling, circuit verification, graph coloring, even the CSPs of the previous section — can be mechanically *encoded* as SAT, so a fast SAT solver is in effect a fast solver for all of them. Second, despite that worst-case hardness, modern solvers routinely dispatch formulas with *millions* of variables in real industrial use. SAT is the rare problem that is provably hard in theory yet astonishingly tractable in practice.

The engine behind classic solvers is **DPLL** (Davis–Putnam–Logemann–Loveland): backtracking search over truth assignments, supercharged with two propagation rules that force assignments instead of guessing them.

- **Unit propagation:** if a clause has every literal false except one still-unassigned literal, that last literal *must* be set true (otherwise the clause is dead). Forcing it often makes another clause unit, triggering a cascade of further forced assignments.
- **Pure literal elimination:** if a variable appears with only one polarity across all remaining clauses (always positive, or always negated), assign it that way — it can only help, never hurt.

```python
def dpll(clauses, assign):
    clauses, assign = unit_propagate(clauses, assign)   # force all unit clauses
    if any(c == [] for c in clauses): return None        # empty clause → conflict
    if not clauses: return assign                        # all satisfied → SAT
    v = choose_unassigned(clauses)
    for val in (True, False):                            # branch on v
        res = dpll(assign_value(clauses, v, val), {**assign, v: val})
        if res is not None: return res
    return None                                          # both fail → backtrack
```

Real-world solvers extend DPLL into **CDCL** (Conflict-Driven Clause Learning). When search hits a conflict — a clause forced empty — the solver doesn't just backtrack blindly; it analyzes *why* the conflict happened, derives a new clause that records that cause, and adds it to the formula so the same dead end is never entered again. It can then jump back many levels at once (*non-chronological backtracking*) rather than undoing one variable at a time. That single idea — learning from failure — is what took SAT from a toy to an industrial workhorse, and it powers tools far beyond logic puzzles: hardware verification, software model checking, and the back ends of **SMT** (Satisfiability Modulo Theories) solvers used throughout program analysis.

::: {.callout-tip}
When you face a gnarly combinatorial feasibility question, a quietly powerful move is: don't write a bespoke solver, *encode it as SAT* (or as a CSP / SMT instance) and hand it to a mature off-the-shelf solver. Decades of engineering in those solvers will usually beat anything you'd write by hand.
:::

## 40.6 — Adversarial search: minimax and alpha-beta pruning

Planning and CSPs assume a cooperative or static world that only you act on. Games break that assumption by adding an **opponent** who actively works against you. **Adversarial search** handles two-player, zero-sum, perfect-information games such as chess, checkers, or tic-tac-toe — *zero-sum* meaning one player's gain is exactly the other's loss, and *perfect-information* meaning both players see the entire state, with no hidden cards or dice.

The core idea is **minimax**. Label the two players MAX (trying to drive the score up) and MIN (trying to drive it down). Build a game tree of possible moves; at the leaves, a single number says how good that position is for MAX. Then back those values up the tree: at a MAX node take the maximum over the children, at a MIN node take the minimum — because each player, when it is their turn, will choose the reply that is best for them. The value that flows all the way up to the root is the game's outcome assuming **both players play optimally**, and the child that produced it is your best move.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  R["MAX → 3"] --> A["MIN → 3"]
  R --> B["MIN → 2"]
  A --> A1["3"]
  A --> A2["5"]
  B --> B1["2"]
  B --> B2["9"]
```

In the tree above, MIN picks the smaller child under each of its nodes (3 on the left, 2 on the right), then MAX picks the larger of those two (3), so MAX moves left — even though the right branch contains the highest leaf (9), MAX can never reach it because MIN would steer away. Formally:

$$ \text{minimax}(s)=\begin{cases} \text{eval}(s) & s \text{ terminal}\\ \max_{a}\ \text{minimax}(\text{result}(s,a)) & s \text{ is a MAX node}\\ \min_{a}\ \text{minimax}(\text{result}(s,a)) & s \text{ is a MIN node}\end{cases} $$

Full game trees are astronomically large — chess has on the order of $10^{120}$ nodes — so two things make minimax usable in practice. First, a **cutoff depth** combined with an **evaluation function**: stop descending at a fixed depth and estimate the non-terminal position with a heuristic (in chess: material count, king safety, pawn structure, mobility). The evaluation function is where most of an engine's playing strength lives, and it is exactly the component that learned approaches later replaced with neural networks. Second, **alpha-beta pruning**: a way to compute the *exact* minimax answer while skipping whole branches that cannot possibly change it.

Alpha-beta carries two bounds down the tree as it searches: $\alpha$, the best score MAX can already guarantee somewhere above, and $\beta$, the best MIN can already guarantee. If at any node $\alpha \ge \beta$, the remaining children are irrelevant — the player to move above would never let the game reach this node — so you **prune** them unexamined.

```python
def alphabeta(node, depth, alpha, beta, maximizing):
    if depth == 0 or node.terminal():
        return node.eval()
    if maximizing:
        value = -inf
        for child in node.children():
            value = max(value, alphabeta(child, depth-1, alpha, beta, False))
            alpha = max(alpha, value)
            if alpha >= beta: break          # β cutoff: MIN won't allow this
        return value
    else:
        value = inf
        for child in node.children():
            value = min(value, alphabeta(child, depth-1, alpha, beta, True))
            beta = min(beta, value)
            if beta <= alpha: break          # α cutoff: MAX won't allow this
        return value
```

Pruning changes nothing about the answer — it returns values identical to plain minimax — but with good **move ordering** (trying the likely-best moves first, so cutoffs trigger early) it cuts the effective branching factor to roughly its square root, letting you search about **twice as deep** in the same time budget. That extra depth is much of what separates a weak engine from a strong one.

::: {.callout-warning}
Minimax assumes a perfectly rational opponent and an exact, fully observable state. Against a fallible human, or in games with chance or hidden information (poker, backgammon), plain minimax is the wrong model — you need *expectiminimax* (which averages over chance nodes), belief states over hidden information, or the sampling methods in the next section.
:::

## 40.7 — Monte Carlo Tree Search: the engine behind AlphaGo

Alpha-beta shines when you have a decent evaluation function and a manageable branching factor. **Go** denies you both: a branching factor near 250, and no good handcrafted way to score a mid-game position — material counting simply doesn't capture who is winning. The breakthrough was to stop trying to evaluate positions analytically and instead **estimate a position's value by simulation**: play many fast games out to the end from that position and see who tends to win. This is **Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)**.

MCTS grows a search tree *asymmetrically*, pouring effort into the most promising lines and barely touching the rest, by repeating four steps thousands or millions of times:

1. **Selection** — starting at the root, descend the existing tree by repeatedly picking the child with the best *selection score*, until you reach a node that still has untried moves.
2. **Expansion** — add one new child node for one of those untried moves.
3. **Simulation (rollout)** — from that new node, play the game out to a terminal result, originally using random or lightly-guided moves.
4. **Backpropagation** — send the result (win or loss) back up the path you visited, updating every node's visit count and win total along the way.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  S["Select<br/>(descend by UCT)"] --> E["Expand<br/>(add a child)"]
  E --> R["Simulate<br/>(rollout to end)"]
  R --> B["Backprop<br/>(update stats)"]
  B --> S
```

The heart of MCTS is the selection rule, **UCT** (Upper Confidence bounds applied to Trees), which resolves the **exploration–exploitation** tension: should you keep playing the move that has been winning (exploit), or try a move you've barely sampled in case it's secretly better (explore)? UCT scores each child by adding the two pressures together:

$$ \text{UCT}(i) = \underbrace{\frac{w_i}{n_i}}_{\text{exploitation}} + c\underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{\ln N}{n_i}}}_{\text{exploration}} $$

Here $w_i$ is the wins accumulated through child $i$, $n_i$ is how many times that child has been visited, $N$ is the parent's visit count, and $c$ is a constant tuning how much you value exploration. A rarely-visited child has a small $n_i$, which inflates the second term, so it eventually gets tried; a child that keeps winning has a large first term, so it keeps getting exploited. As simulations pile up these averages converge, and the move you actually play is the *most-visited* child of the root (a more stable choice than the highest average).

```python
import math
def uct(child, parent_visits, c=1.41):
    if child.n == 0:
        return float('inf')                 # always try an unvisited child first
    exploit = child.w / child.n             # average outcome so far
    explore = c * math.sqrt(math.log(parent_visits) / child.n)
    return exploit + explore
```

MCTS has three properties that explain why it spread far beyond Go. It is **anytime** — stop whenever the clock runs out and play the best move found so far. It needs **no evaluation function** — the rollouts supply value estimates for free. And it is **aheuristic** — it works on any game where you can simulate to a result, so it became a default for general game playing and even some planning problems. **AlphaGo** then fused MCTS with deep learning: a *policy network* proposed which moves were worth exploring (replacing random rollouts with sharp move priors), and a *value network* estimated who was winning (replacing or augmenting the full rollouts). **AlphaZero** pushed this to its logical end, learning both networks from self-play alone with no human games, using MCTS as the search backbone — the clearest demonstration that classical search and modern learning are partners, not rivals.

## 40.8 — Key takeaways

- **Planning** finds a sequence of actions from an initial state to a goal; **STRIPS/PDDL** model each action as precondition/add/delete lists, and the STRIPS assumption (unmentioned facts persist) sidesteps the frame problem — turning planning into search over states.
- Plan search runs **forward** (real states, big branching, delete-relaxation heuristics), **backward** (goal-relevant actions only), **partial-order** (least commitment, exposes parallelism), or **hierarchical** (HTN, encodes expert decomposition).
- A **CSP** is variables + domains + constraints over a constraint graph; solve it with **backtracking** plus **constraint propagation** (forward checking, arc consistency / AC-3) and ordering heuristics like **MRV** and least-constraining-value.
- **SAT** is the canonical NP-complete CSP; **DPLL** with unit propagation and pure-literal elimination, extended to **CDCL** clause learning, makes million-variable instances tractable and underpins verification and SMT.
- **Minimax** solves two-player zero-sum games by backing up each player's best reply; **alpha-beta pruning** returns the identical answer while searching about twice as deep.
- **MCTS** estimates value by simulation and balances exploration/exploitation via **UCT**; combined with deep policy/value networks it became **AlphaGo / AlphaZero**.
- The unifying theme across all three: structure the space, then use that structure — relaxations, propagation, pruning, sampling — to avoid ever exploring the parts you don't need.

## 40.9 — See also

- **Chapter 38 — Search & Problem Solving:** uninformed and heuristic search (BFS, A\*, admissible heuristics) that planning and CSP solving build directly upon.
- **Chapter 39 — Knowledge Representation & Reasoning:** the logical machinery behind states, goals, the closed-world assumption, and the frame problem.
- **Chapter 41 — Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics:** alternative, stochastic ways to search huge combinatorial spaces when exact methods stall.
- **Chapter 23 — Reinforcement Learning Foundations:** the learning-based counterpart to planning, where the world model and value come from experience rather than a given specification.
- **Chapter 25 — Deep Reinforcement Learning:** how policy and value networks (as in AlphaZero) are trained, fusing search with learned evaluation.
- **Chapter 26 — Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory:** the broader theory of optimal play in games beyond two-player zero-sum.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 39 · 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning](ch39.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [41 · 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics →](ch41.html)
